title: Rails master keys should not be disclosed
description: In most cases, trust boundaries are violated when a secret is exposed in a source code repository or an uncontrolled deployment environment. Unintended people who don’t need to know the secret might get access to it. They might then be able to use it to gain unwanted access to associated services or resources.
recommendation: Review and fix according to the rule guidelines.

checks.message: Rails Master Key detected. Rails master keys are 32-character hexadecimal strings used to encrypt and decrypt credentials stored in config/credentials.yml.enc and other encrypted configuration files in Ruby on Rails applications. This master key provides access to all encrypted secrets including database passwords, API keys, payment gateway credentials, third-party service tokens, encryption keys, and other sensitive configuration data. Exposing this master key allows attackers to decrypt all application secrets, access production databases, hijack third-party service integrations, compromise user data, manipulate payment transactions, impersonate the application in external services, and gain complete control over your Rails application's sensitive configuration. Immediately regenerate the master key by deleting config/master.key and running 'rails credentials:edit' to create a new master key, re-encrypt all credentials with the new key, rotate all secrets stored in the credentials file (database passwords, API keys, etc.), update deployment pipelines and servers with the new RAILS_MASTER_KEY environment variable, audit application logs and database access for unauthorized activity during the exposure window, review all encrypted credentials for potential compromise, implement proper secret storage using environment variables or secret management systems like AWS Secrets Manager or HashiCorp Vault, never commit master.key to version control (ensure it's in .gitignore), use different master keys for each environment (development, staging, production), and implement monitoring and alerting for unusual credential access patterns.

Rails Master Key detected in variable assignment. Rails uses master keys to encrypt sensitive configuration data in credentials.yml.enc files, which typically contain database passwords, API keys, payment processor credentials, email service tokens, cloud storage keys, third-party integration secrets, and other critical application secrets. This 32-character hexadecimal key is the single point of failure for your application's security - anyone with access to this key can decrypt all encrypted credentials and gain complete access to your application's integrated services and data stores. Exposing the master key enables attackers to decrypt production database credentials, access payment processing accounts, hijack email services, compromise cloud storage, manipulate authentication systems, access admin panels and internal tools, and potentially pivot to other systems using the exposed credentials. Immediately regenerate your Rails master key by removing the old config/master.key file and running 'EDITOR=nano rails credentials:edit' to generate a new one, re-encrypt all credentials with the new master key, systematically rotate every secret stored in your credentials file starting with the most critical (database credentials, payment keys, etc.), update all deployment environments (production, staging) with the new RAILS_MASTER_KEY environment variable, thoroughly audit database access logs and application logs for the timeframe when the key was exposed, review third-party service access logs (Stripe, SendGrid, AWS, etc.) for unauthorized activity, implement comprehensive secret management using tools like AWS Secrets Manager, HashiCorp Vault, or Azure Key Vault, ensure master.key is always in .gitignore and never committed to version control, use separate master keys for each environment, implement secrets rotation policies, enable audit logging for credential access, and consider implementing additional encryption layers for highly sensitive data.
